data.title: Winter Storm Blankets Central and Eastern U.S. — WSJ
data.description:
By Alyssa Lukpat, Joseph Pisani and Collin Eaton

A massive winter storm is disrupting swaths of the U.S., dropping snow, freezing roads and causing thousands of flight cancellations and power outages.

More than 200 million people were in the path of the storm, which is straining the nation's power grid and travel infrastructure. The storm already has been blamed for roughly 11,300 flight cancellations, affecting airports from Dallas to Boston, and has left more than 920,000 power customers in the dark, mainly in the South.

The outages hit hardest in Tennessee, where almost 300,000 customers lost power. Nearly 200,000 of those were in the Nashville area. The Nashville Electric Service said the outages could last "days or longer," although the utility will mobilize scores of line workers throughout the day.

At King's Hill House of Prayer, on a hill overlooking Nashville, Crystal Nelson, an intercessory missionary, said her community of missionaries lost power around 7:30 a.m. Without a generator, Nelson's community is considering huddling together in their warmest rooms. Nelson, a Floridian until she moved to Nashville in November, said this ice storm is her first.

"There are trees down everywhere. We couldn't get out if we wanted to," she said. "This isn't usual for Nashville, so they're not all that prepared."

The storm began to lash the central and southern parts of the country with snow on Saturday and the Northeast on Sunday. Meteorologists said to expect heavy snow, sleet and freezing rain. Some regions, including parts of the Northeast, could expect about 2 feet of snow, according to the National Weather Service. Some areas declared states of emergency ahead of the storm.

Forecasters said the storm would stretch 2,000 miles from New Mexico to Maine. "It's rare to have that wide of an expanse," said David Hamrick, a meteorologist at the National Weather Service.

The snowfall was set to end later on Sunday, but frigid temperatures should keep things icy for the rest of the week, Hamrick said. Forecasters said the cold could be life-threatening and warned people to avoid traveling.

In Texas, many residents worried that this weekend's frigid temperatures and ice would bring power outages similar to 2021, when a rare winter storm crippled the state's power grid and left millions without power for almost a week. In Houston on Saturday, grocery stores were packed with people. The stores were low on supplies such as bottled water and firewood, as the current winter storm evoked memories of the prolonged blackout five years ago.

So far, most power in Texas has stayed on. About 100,000 customers were left in the dark as of Sunday afternoon, largely near the state's eastern border with Louisiana, a harder-hit state where more than 120,000 had lost power. Houston, Dallas, Austin and their surrounding suburbs in Texas only had about 2,000 power customers without electricity among them.

Flight cancellations this weekend are surging as the storm moves northeast. As of Sunday morning, more than 10,000 flights in or out of U.S. airports had been canceled and over 1,000 had been delayed, according to flight-data provider FlightAware.

American Airlines had scrapped more than 1,400 flights, representing nearly half of its schedule.

Delta Air Lines had removed about 40% of its Sunday schedule, while United Airlines and Southwest Airlines had scrapped about one-third of their flights. Low-cost carrier JetBlue Airways had scrubbed more than 70% of its flights, while more than 40% of Spirit Airlines flights and 30% of Frontier Airlines flights had been canceled.

Most flights had been canceled out of airports servicing Atlanta; Boston; Charlotte, N.C.; New York City; Philadelphia; and Washington, D.C. About 1,900 flights scheduled for Monday have been canceled pre-emptively.

Updates to follow as news develops.

Write to Alyssa Lukpat at alyssa.lukpat@wsj.com, Joseph Pisani at joseph.pisani@wsj.com and Collin Eaton at collin.eaton@wsj.com

data.title: Federal officials dig in on Minneapolis shooting narrative, contradicting video evidence
data.description:
Key points:
  • Video evidence clashes with official version of events
  • Minneapolis police chief says nothing indicates Pretti brandished gun
  • Federal agents have killed two US citizens in Minneapolis this month

By Brad Brooks and Tim Reid

Senior Trump administration officials on Sunday defended the fatal shooting of a U.S. citizen by immigration agents in Minneapolis even as video evidence contradicted their version of events and tensions grew between local law enforcement and federal officers.

As residents visited a makeshift shrine of flowers and candles in frigid temperatures and snow to mark Saturday's fatal shooting of Alex Pretti — the second shooting death involving federal officers in Minneapolis this month — Trump administration officials stated that Pretti assaulted officers, compelling them to fire in self-defense.

"The victims are border patrol agents," Gregory Bovino, Border Patrol commander-at-large, told CNN's "State of the Union" program.

That official line, echoed by Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem and other members of the Trump administration on Sunday, triggered outrage among local law enforcement, many Minneapolis residents and Democrats on Capitol Hill, who pointed to bystander videos that appear to show a different version of events.

HOLDING A PHONE, NOT A GUN

Videos from the scene verified and reviewed by Reuters showed Pretti, 37, holding a phone in his hand, not a gun, as he tries to help other protesters who had been pushed to the ground by agents.

As the videos begin, Pretti can be seen filming while a federal agent pushes away one woman and shoves another woman to the ground. Pretti moves between the agent and the women, then raises his left arm to shield himself as the agent pepper sprays him.

Several agents then take hold of Pretti — who struggles with them — and force him onto his hands and knees. As the agents pin Pretti down, someone shouts what sounds like a warning about the presence of a gun.

Video footage then appears to show one of the agents removing a gun from Pretti and stepping away from the group with it.

Moments later, an officer with a handgun points at Pretti's back and fires four shots in quick succession. Several more shots can then be heard as another agent appears to fire at Pretti.

Darius Reeves, the former head of ICE's field office in Baltimore, told Reuters that federal agents' apparent lack of communication was troubling. "It's clear no one is communicating to me, based on my observation of how that team responded," Reeves said.

He drew attention to signs that an officer appeared to have taken possession of Pretti's weapon before he was killed. "The proof to me is how everyone scatters," he said. "They're looking around, trying to figure out where the shots came from."

'VIDEOS SPEAK FOR THEMSELVES'

Brian O'Hara, the Minneapolis police chief, told the CBS "Face the Nation" program that "the videos speak for themselves," calling the Trump administration's version of events deeply disturbing. He said he had seen no evidence that Pretti brandished a gun.

Tensions in the city were already running high after a federal agent fatally shot U.S. citizen Renee Good on January 7. Trump officials said she was trying to ram the agent with her car but other observers have said bystander video suggests she was trying to steer away from the officer who shot her.

Federal authorities have refused to allow local officials to participate in their investigation of the incident.

Chief executives of some of Minnesota's largest companies, including Target, Cargill and Best Buy, published a letter calling for the "immediate de-escalation of tensions and for state, local and federal officials to work together to find real solutions." They did not condemn Pretti's shooting.

Former Democratic President Barack Obama and former First Lady Michelle Obama issued a statement saying many of America's core values were under assault. "This has to stop," they said.

The deaths of Good and Pretti have sparked large protests in the Democrat-run city, although on Sunday morning the area where Pretti had been shot was calm.

A woman wearing nursing scrubs ventured out in Sunday's frigid temperatures to pay homage to Pretti, who she said worked with her. When asked what brought her out, the woman began to sob.

"He was caring and he was kind. None of this makes any sense," said the woman, who asked not to be identified by name, saying she feared retribution from the federal government.

In addition to large protests in Minneapolis since Good's death, there have been rallies in other cities led by Democratic politicians, including Los Angeles and Washington, D.C., since Trump began sending immigration agents and National Guard troops to those communities last year.

Trump has defended the operations as necessary to reduce crime and enforce immigration laws.

U.S. Senator Amy Klobuchar, a Democrat from Minnesota who has filed to run for governor, told the ABC News "This Week" program that Trump's surge of federal agents into Minneapolis was "completely out of control and out of balance," and that they should leave Minnesota. She described the shooting of Pretti as "simply horrific."

Pretti's shooting triggered legal filings on Saturday night from state and local officials, as well as others.

A U.S. district judge issued a temporary restraining order prohibiting federal officials from destroying or altering evidence related to the shooting, in response to a lawsuit filed by Minnesota's attorney general, the Hennepin County Attorney’s Office and the Bureau of Criminal Apprehension. A full hearing is set for Monday.

Lawyers representing protesters in Minnesota also asked an appeals court to reinstate a lower court's order that prevented violent retaliation by federal agents against protesters, citing Pretti's death and the likelihood of a surge of people taking to the streets.

data.title: One Million Without Power, Flight Cancellations Soar as Winter Storm Cripples U.S. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Janet H. Cho

Airlines canceled more than 10,700 flights into and out of U.S. airports by 1:30 p.m. Eastern time, and more than 989,000 households were without power on Sunday as a winter storm barreled across a wide swath of the U.S.

More than 1,000 inbound and outbound flights were canceled at Charlotte Douglas and Atlanta's Hartsfield-Jackson International Airports, according to FlightAware.com's live flight-tracking site. Dallas-Fort Worth, John F. Kennedy Jr., and Newark Liberty Airports had each canceled more than 900 flights.

Sunday's flight disruptions come on the top of the 4,100 flights canceled on Saturday, and carriers have already canceled 2,000 flights for Monday.

The storm has left more than 989,500 households without power in 10 states across the South and Southeast, including 337,000 in Tennessee, 157,000 in Mississippi, 139,000 in Louisiana, and 100,000 in Texas, according to PowerOutage.us.

Airports from Albuquerque, N.M., to Hartford, Conn., are deicing planes, and Atlanta's airport was reporting average delays of 44 minutes, the Federal Aviation Administration reported. Twenty-seven other airports have closed entirely.

American Airlines canceled more than 1,500 flights, or 47% of its daily schedule, according to FlightAware.com. Delta Air Lines canceled 1,381 flights, or 42% of its daily schedule; Southwest Airlines canceled 1,316 flights, or 33% of its daily schedule; United Airlines canceled 997 fights, or 36% of its daily schedule; and Republic Airways, a regional carrier that operates service for American, Delta, and United, canceled 900 flights, or 87% of its schedule.

Surface travel wasn't much better, as snow blanketed the Northeast after dumping on the South earlier this weekend. New Jersey Transit is suspending weekend all bus, light rain, and rail service at 2 p.m. Eastern time until conditions allow for safe resumption.

Amtrak said it was canceling some trains because of the dangerous weather conditions, and said customers can rebook or request refunds without penalties at Amtrack.com, on the Amtrak mobile app, or by calling 1-800-USA-RAIL.

The National Weather Service said a broad swath of heavy snow, sleet, and freezing rain stretching from the Southern Rockies to New England will continue through Monday, followed by bitterly cold air and dangerously cold wind chills that will prolong dangerous travel conditions throughout the week.

New England states will see up to 18 inches of snow, areas around the Great Lakes will see major lake-effect snow, and parts of the Mid-Atlantic and Ohio and Tennessee Valleys will see half an inch of freezing rain through Monday morning, NWS said. The Central Gulf Coast will get showers and severe thunderstorms through Monday morning, including a greater risk of frequent lightning, severe thunderstorm wind gusts, and tornadoes.

data.title: Canada's Carney Ditches 'Mr. Nice Guy' Approach to Trump — WSJ
data.description:
By Paul Vieira

OTTAWA — Prime Minister Mark Carney came into office almost a year ago trying to build warm relations with President Trump. Now, he's playing hardball.

By resolving a trade dispute with China and issuing a call-to-arms against economic coercion at Davos, Switzerland, the former central-banker governor known for his calculated caution is taking Canada into a high-stakes gambit that could put its economy in an even tighter squeeze, political analysts said.

He may not have a choice, the analysts said. Carney has tried to appease Trump with strengthened border security, fentanyl-trafficking deterrence, ramping up military spending and dropping a digital tax affecting U.S. tech companies. None of it has worked, and he needs leverage going into negotiations this year to redo the U.S.-Mexico-Canada free-trade agreement, or USMCA, that underpins the country's economy.

Carney "has tried to be nice, but he hasn't gotten too far," said Christopher Sands, director of Canadian studies at Johns Hopkins University. "Carney is now turning up the heat to get more attention."

Carney has said little about why he is making a U-turn in his Trump strategy, but analysts pointed to two emerging reasons.

First, projecting strength could give Carney some bargaining chips before USMCA talks, analysts said, much as China tried to get the upper hand on Trump with new export restrictions on critical minerals ahead of a summit with Chinese leader Xi Jinping.

Fen Hampson, an international politics professor at Ottawa's Carleton University, said Carney has decided "it's time to take the gloves off and play hardball."

Hampson said the timing of Canada's trade resolution with China "is sending a strong message to Washington. You don't want to play ball with us. Well, guess what, the rest of the world does, and the rest of the world is our oyster."

Second, David Coletto, president of Ottawa-based polling firm Abacus Data, said Carney's speech in Davos could work as a setup for a possible snap election to secure a majority mandate in Parliament. The Carney-led Liberals are a minority government, which means they could lose power if opposition parties unite against them.

"In the world Carney is describing, stability becomes an asset," Coletto said. "So if Carney wanted to use this Davos frame as the lead-in to an early election, the cleaner message probably isn't 'give me a majority,' it's 'give Canada stability.'"

Beyond China, Carney has tried to create stronger ties with Europe and Persian Gulf states. In Qatar, shortly following his China breakthrough, Carney said he secured investment pledges from the Gulf state in Canadian clean-energy projects, artificial-intelligence ventures, and the defense sector. Canada has also gained access to a European Union defense fund, to reduce dependence on the U.S.

"Carney wants to show he can get things done without waiting around for Donald Trump to solve Canada's problems," Sands said.

Carney is scheduled to visit Australia in March. Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said he agreed with the thrust of Carney's speech at Davos, which called on smaller powers like Canada and Australia to work in unison to push back against economic coercion from global hegemons — although the speech never mentioned Trump.

The moves have sparked a backlash from the Trump administration, which has turned U.S. policy toward an emphasis on dominating the Western Hemisphere.

Trump, in a Saturday Truth Social post, warned Canada he would slap 100% tariffs on all Canadian imports should Ottawa clinch a free-trade deal with Beijing. Trump also referred to Carney as Canada's "governor," a moniker he used frequently with the former premier, Justin Trudeau, but not so with Carney. Trump has previously mused about annexing Canada as the 51st state.

On Sunday, Carney told reporters that he "has no intention to pursue free-trade agreements" with China or other nonmarket economies. The pact with China is about rectifying a tariff dispute regarding Canadian agricultural and food products, and China-made electric vehicles, he said.

Trump shot back on Truth Social Sunday that "China is successfully and completely taking over the once Great Country of Canada."

Carney's relationship with Trump started out friendly. Since coming to power last year, Carney has communicated regularly with Trump, via texts and phone calls. In the Oval Office last fall, Trump, sitting next to Carney, called the Canadian leader "a world-class leader...He does a great job and he's a tough negotiator."

At that time, Carney and Canadian officials were trying to strike a deal with Washington that would sharply reduce tariffs of up to 50% on Canadian imports such as steel, aluminum and motor vehicles. Trump terminated those talks weeks later, angered about an ad paid for by the province of Ontario featuring audio from former Republican President Ronald Reagan criticizing tariffs.

Carney and Canadian officials tried to get those talks back on track, with the prime minister apologizing to Trump for the Reagan ads. However, Carney later acknowledged the sector-specific tariffs were here to stay, and Canada would seek relief during the USMCA talks.

Canada's economy is struggling under the weight of those U.S. tariffs, and business and household trepidation given uncertainty over Trump's trade policy and USMCA talks. Historically, roughly 20% of Canada's gross domestic product is tied to trade with the U.S., making the country, along with Mexico, the most vulnerable to Trump's protectionism.

Mark Warner, a Toronto trade lawyer who practices in Canada and the U.S., said he wants to be optimistic that USMCA will remain largely intact after talks this year, "but I don't think Carney can keep going like this because it will be impossible to renegotiate."

Carney's main domestic political rival, Conservative Party Leader Pierre Poilievre, praised portions of the prime minister's Davos address on the need for Canada to be more self-reliant. But he criticized Carney for failing to get tangible results for the economy. "We have had enough words," he said." Now, we need results."

In a video Carney released on X Saturday, hours after Trump's 100% tariff threat, he said he's trying to lead a transformation focused on Canadian citizens controlling their economic destiny amid "threats from abroad."

Some political watchers said this sounded like a pre-election ad.

Write to Paul Vieira at Paul.Vieira@wsj.com
